questions.question: Where does a classifier layer like Llama Guard sit, and why is that placement powerful but risky?
options: Inside the model weights; it cannot be bypassed
At the narrowest point of the stack — every input and output passes through — so it catches obvious misuse cheaply, but a weak classifier becomes a false sense of security
Between the GPU and the CPU
questions.explanation: The choke point means a small compute cost screens everything. That leverage cuts both ways: it's why classifiers are valuable AND why over-trusting them is dangerous — they're a layer, not a solution.

questions.question: What is the "product" of Llama Guard — the thing downstream systems actually wire actions to?
options: The hazard taxonomy (S1 Violent Crimes through S13/S14) — a shared vocabulary letting systems block some categories, flag others for review, and annotate-but-allow the rest
questions.explanation: Llama Guard 3 uses the MLCommons 13-hazard taxonomy across 8 languages; Guard 4 adds multimodal input and S14 Code Interpreter Abuse — directly relevant to sandboxed coding agents. Category-specific routing is the integration point.

questions.question: What does NeMo Guardrails' dialog-rail layer add over input/output rails?
options: Colang-defined flow constraints across turns — e.g. "do not discuss medical diagnosis in a support bot even if asked three different ways" — versus single-turn classify-and-block
questions.explanation: Input/output rails judge one turn; dialog rails enforce multi-turn flow policy. NeMo also composes Llama Guard, Prompt Guard, and custom classifiers under one framework.

questions.question: What did Huang et al. (2025) demonstrate about guardrail classifiers?
options: They achieve 100% accuracy on all attacks
Emoji Smuggling reached 100% attack success on six prominent guard systems, and NeMo Guard Detect recorded 72.54% ASR on crafted jailbreaks — classifiers are a layer, not a solution
Classifiers are unnecessary with modern models
questions.explanation: Character-level attacks (emoji smuggling, homoglyph substitution), in-context redirection, and semantic paraphrase all measurably drop classifier accuracy. The ceiling is clearly not zero ASR, which is why classifiers pair with constitutional behavior and HITL.

questions.question: Given their bypass rates, what do classifiers still legitimately win at?
options: Fast default rejection of obvious misuse, category routing for differential handling, output rails catching leaked categories, and an auditable compliance surface with a declared taxonomy
questions.explanation: A CSAM request is caught in milliseconds; categories route to block/log/escalate; output rails catch what the model would otherwise leak; and a documented taxonomy is a real regulatory surface. They reduce blast radius cheaply — they just can't be the only defense.
